characters.name: Mr. Bennet
characters.description: Father of five daughters, characterized as an odd mixture of quick parts, sarcastic humour, reserve, and caprice who takes delight in teasing his wife.

characters.name: Mrs. Bennet
characters.description: Mr. Bennet's wife, a woman of mean understanding, little information, and uncertain temper whose main business in life is getting her daughters married.

physical.appearance: formerly beautiful, has five grown-up daughters

characters.name: Mr. Bingley
characters.description: A single young man of large fortune from the north of England who has taken Netherfield Park.

physical.appearance: good-looking, pleasant countenance, wears blue coat, rides black horse

characters.name: Lizzy
characters.description: One of the Bennet daughters, favored by her father who notes she has more quickness than her sisters.

physical.appearance: not as handsome as Jane according to her mother

characters.name: Jane
characters.description: One of the Bennet daughters, described by her mother as more handsome than Lizzy.

physical.appearance: handsome, most beautiful creature, only handsome girl in the room according to Mr. Darcy

characters.name: Lydia
characters.description: One of the Bennet daughters, described by her mother as more good-humoured than Lizzy.

characters.name: Mrs. Long
characters.description: A neighbor who brought news to Mrs. Bennet about Netherfield Park being let.

characters.name: Mr. Morris
characters.description: The person with whom Mr. Bingley agreed terms for Netherfield Park.

characters.name: Sir William Lucas
characters.description: A neighbor who, along with his wife, is determined to visit Mr. Bingley.

characters.name: Lady Lucas
characters.description: Sir William's wife, who is determined to visit Mr. Bingley despite generally not visiting newcomers.

characters.name: Elizabeth
characters.description: One of the Bennet daughters, Mr. Bennet's second daughter who was trimming a hat.

physical.appearance: tolerable but not handsome enough according to Mr. Darcy

characters.name: Kitty
characters.description: One of the Bennet daughters who has a persistent cough and lacks discretion in her coughing.

characters.name: Mary
characters.description: One of the Bennet daughters, described as a young lady of deep reflection who reads great books and makes extracts.

characters.name: Miss Bingley
characters.description: One of Mr. Bingley's sisters, a fine woman with an air of decided fashion.

physical.appearance: fine woman with an air of decided fashion

characters.name: Mrs. Hurst
characters.description: Mr. Bingley's eldest sister, a fine woman with an air of decided fashion.

physical.appearance: fine woman with an air of decided fashion, elegant dress with lace

characters.name: Mr. Hurst
characters.description: The husband of Mr. Bingley's eldest sister, who merely looks the gentleman.

characters.name: Mr. Darcy
characters.description: Mr. Bingley's friend, a wealthy man with ten thousand a year and an estate in Derbyshire.

physical.appearance: fine, tall person, handsome features, noble mien, much handsomer than Mr. Bingley

characters.name: Miss Lucas
characters.description: Daughter of Sir William and Lady Lucas, danced with Mr. Bingley at the assembly.

characters.name: Maria Lucas
characters.description: Daughter of Sir William and Lady Lucas, danced with Mr. Bingley at the assembly.

characters.name: Miss King
characters.description: A young lady who danced with Mr. Bingley at the assembly.

timeline.event: Mrs. Long visits Mrs. Bennet and informs her that Netherfield Park has been let
Mr. Bingley comes down on Monday in a chaise and four to see Netherfield Park
Mr. Bingley agrees with Mr. Morris to take Netherfield Park
Mrs. Bennet tells Mr. Bennet the news about Mr. Bingley and Netherfield Park
Mrs. Bennet tries to convince Mr. Bennet to visit Mr. Bingley when he arrives
Mr. Bennet secretly visits Mr. Bingley in the morning
Mr. Bennet reveals to his family that he has visited Mr. Bingley while observing Elizabeth trimming a hat
The family discusses plans for the upcoming ball in a fortnight and when to invite Mr. Bingley to dinner
The Bennet family questions Mr. Bennet about Mr. Bingley but he evades giving details
Lady Lucas provides favorable intelligence about Mr. Bingley from Sir William's visit
Mr. Bingley returns Mr. Bennet's visit and sits with him in his library for ten minutes
The Bennet ladies observe Mr. Bingley from an upper window, noting his blue coat and black horse
The Bennets send a dinner invitation to Mr. Bingley
Mr. Bingley declines the dinner invitation due to business in town
Reports circulate that Mr. Bingley will bring twelve ladies and seven gentlemen to the assembly
The actual party size is corrected to six people from London - five sisters and a cousin
The Netherfield party arrives at the assembly consisting of five people: Mr. Bingley, his two sisters, Mr. Hurst, and Mr. Darcy
Mr. Darcy's wealth of ten thousand a year becomes known within five minutes of his entrance
Mr. Bingley dances every dance and makes himself acquainted with all principal people in the room
Mr. Darcy dances only with Mrs. Hurst and Miss Bingley, declining other introductions
Elizabeth sits out two dances due to scarcity of gentlemen
Mr. Bingley asks Mr. Darcy to dance and suggests he dance with Elizabeth
Mr. Darcy calls Elizabeth tolerable but not handsome enough to tempt him
Mr. Bingley dances with Jane twice and she is admired by his sisters
Mary is mentioned to Miss Bingley as the most accomplished girl in the neighbourhood
Catherine and Lydia are never without partners at the ball
The family returns to Longbourn and Mrs. Bennet recounts the evening's events to Mr. Bennet

settings.name: Bennet household
settings.description: The family home where Mr. and Mrs. Bennet have their conversation about the new neighbor and their five daughters.

settings.name: Netherfield Park
settings.description: An estate that has been let to Mr. Bingley, a young man of large fortune from the north of England. He is to take possession before Michaelmas.

settings.name: The neighbourhood
settings.description: A rural community where families like the Bennets, the Lucases, and Mrs. Long reside, where news of newcomers spreads quickly among the families.

settings.name: The assemblies
settings.description: Local social gatherings where the community meets and dances, where the Bennets expect to meet Mr. Bingley.

settings.name: Mr. Bennet's library
settings.description: The room in the Bennet household where Mr. Bennet receives Mr. Bingley's visit and where he reads books regardless of time.

settings.name: Longbourn
settings.description: The village where the Bennet family lives and of which they are the principal inhabitants.

settings.name: Assembly-room
settings.description: The venue where the local ball takes place, where the Netherfield party makes their public debut in the neighborhood.

settings.name: London
settings.description: The city where Mr. Bingley has business and from where he brings his party to the assembly.

settings.name: Hertfordshire
settings.description: The county where Netherfield Park is located and where the story takes place.

settings.name: Derbyshire
settings.description: The county where Mr. Darcy has his large estate.

world_rules: A single man in possession of a good fortune must be in want of a wife
Families consider wealthy single men as rightful property for one of their daughters
Social protocol requires formal visits to new neighbors, particularly from the gentleman of the house
It is impossible for ladies to visit a new gentleman neighbor if their husband/father has not first paid a formal visit
Parents, especially mothers, are expected to arrange advantageous marriages for their daughters
A fortnight's acquaintance is considered very little time to truly know someone
Once a formal visit has been paid, the acquaintance cannot be escaped
Social introductions at public gatherings like assemblies require proper connections
Being fond of dancing is considered a certain step towards falling in love
Wealth and social status are quickly circulated as gossip in small communities
A gentleman must be particularly acquainted with a lady before he will dance with her at an assembly
Ladies may observe gentlemen callers from upper windows without being seen
Social manners and behavior at public gatherings quickly determine one's reputation in the community

voice_profile.style_notes: Opens with famous ironic universal truth, uses free indirect discourse, sharp dialogue that reveals character, social commentary through narrative voice

plot_threads.thread: Will Mr. Bennet visit Mr. Bingley to enable social introductions for his daughters?
Which of the Bennet daughters might catch Mr. Bingley's romantic interest?
Will Mrs. Bennet succeed in her primary mission of getting her daughters married?
When will Mr. Bingley return Mr. Bennet's visit and when will the Bennets invite him to dinner?
Will Mr. Bingley dance with the Bennet daughters at the upcoming ball?
How will the relationship between Mr. Bingley and Jane develop?
How will Elizabeth's first impression of Mr. Darcy affect their future interactions?
Will Mr. Darcy's proud behavior continue to make him unpopular in the neighborhood?
Will Mr. Bingley give a ball at Netherfield as he mentioned?
